metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the plan to cover updating `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py` (`read_module_size_allowlist` parser and the per-file ceiling comparison logic) alongside the `test.yml`/`gerrit-verify.yaml` shell gate changes, so all three consumers of the allowlist format are updated atomically.
findings.finding: The plan changes the allowlist format to `path <max-lines>` rows but omits the existing Python consumer `read_module_size_allowlist()` in `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py`, which parses bare-path lines. After the format change, the three structural tests in that file will break (entries like `src/rebar/config.py 1310` won't match real filesystem paths), yet the plan's AC and Remediation section only reference `test.yml`.
findings.location: Remediation section / Acceptance Criteria; tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py:47-54 (`read_module_size_allowlist`), :77-86 (`test_every_allowlist_entry_is_a_real_file`), :57-73 (`test_allowlist_equals_computed_over_cap_set`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan step: 'Update `read_module_size_allowlist()` in `test_module_size_contract.py` to parse `path <max-lines>` rows, returning a `dict[str, int]`. Update `test_allowlist_equals_computed_over_cap_set` to check per-file ceilings (not just set equality) and `test_every_allowlist_entry_is_a_real_file` to extract the path token before `is_file()`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` as an explicit edit target: its module-size gate block (lines 151-165) must be updated to the same per-file-ceiling parsing logic as test.yml.
findings.finding: `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` contains a verbatim copy of the module-size gate shell block (lines 151-165, identical logic to test.yml lines 179-201) and reads the same allowlist, but the plan does not name it as an edit target. After the format change, the gerrit gate will silently stop working correctly (its `grep -vE` parser reads bare paths; `path <max-lines>` rows would be treated as non-matching entries).
findings.location: Remediation section / .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml lines 151-165
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'File impact' entry for `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml` and ensure its gate shell block is updated identically to the test.yml gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the canonical `path <max-lines>` field format (separator, field indices) and specify updated parsing logic for ALL three consumers: `test.yml`, `gerrit-verify.yaml`, and `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py:read_module_size_allowlist()`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `path <max-lines>` allowlist row format (its core schema change) but never defines the field separator or parsing protocol, leaving three concrete consumers under-specified: `test.yml` (shell `comm -23`), `gerrit-verify.yaml` (identical shell logic), and `test_module_size_contract.py` (`read_module_size_allowlist()` returns bare stripped lines). An executor cannot implement the gate without inventing the format.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Convert the allowlist from bare filenames to `path <max-lines>` rows'
findings.suggested_fix: Choose a canonical separator (e.g., single space) and document it as the allowlist format spec. Provide a sed/awk snippet for the shell consumers (e.g., `awk '{print $1}'` to extract path, `awk '{print $2}'` for ceiling) and show the updated `read_module_size_allowlist()` signature returning `dict[str, int]` instead of `set[str]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py` as an explicit edit target: update `read_module_size_allowlist()` to parse the new `path <max-lines>` format and extend the test assertions to cover the per-file ceiling check.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py` parses the allowlist via `read_module_size_allowlist()` which strips each line and returns bare strings (a `set[str]`). Changing the allowlist format to `path <max-lines>` rows breaks this parser: `test_every_allowlist_entry_is_a_real_file` would try to resolve `"src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py 821"` as a file path (it won't exist), and `test_allowlist_equals_computed_over_cap_set` would see all old entries as stale. The plan does not name this test file.
findings.location: Remediation section / tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py lines 47-86
findings.suggested_fix: Update `read_module_size_allowlist()` to return a `dict[str, int]` mapping path→ceiling, and add a new assertion in `test_allowlist_equals_computed_over_cap_set` that each allowlisted path's actual LOC ≤ its recorded ceiling.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for AC2 that temporarily adds a test allowlist entry with a ceiling below the file's actual LOC, runs the gate script locally (or in a dry-run CI step), and asserts a non-zero exit code.
findings.finding: AC2 ('test.yml fails when an allowlisted file grows past its ceiling; shrinking is free') has no concrete proving command. The gate logic is the riskiest change — a subtly wrong awk/comm expression could silently pass a grown file. No step describes how to exercise the failure path (e.g., temporarily inflate a test file, run the gate, confirm non-zero exit).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'Proving command: echo "src/foo.py 1" >> .github/module-size-allowlist.txt && bash .github/check-module-size.sh; assert exit code != 0; then revert. Also assert exit code == 0 after reverting to confirm shrink passes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether a `__init__.py` exemption actually exists in the current gate; if it does not, remove AC-3 and the corresponding Remediation sentence to avoid specifying work against a phantom feature.
findings.finding: The Remediation section specifies 'drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption' and AC-3 requires '`__init__.py` is gate-visible (pinned row, not filename-exempt)', but the actual gate code in `test.yml` (lines 183-187) and `gerrit-verify.yaml` (lines 155-159) contains no `__init__.py` exemption — the only exclusion is `grep -v __pycache__`. This remediation step targets a phantom feature, making AC-3 trivially pre-satisfied and introducing unnecessary churn.
findings.location: Remediation section ('drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption') / AC-3; .github/workflows/test.yml lines 183-187; .github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml lines 155-159

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and correct the plan's claim that the current gate has a `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption — reading `test.yml` lines 183-187 and `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` shows no such exemption exists; remove or rephrase the Remediation bullet and AC3 accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan's Remediation section asserts 'drop the __init__.py filename-pattern exemption (give it a pinned ceiling row instead) so it is gate-visible', implying the current gate already exempts __init__.py files by filename pattern. Reading the actual gate scripts (test.yml lines 183-187, gerrit-verify.yaml lines 155-159), no such exemption exists — the find/awk/comm logic treats all *.py files identically (only __pycache__ dirs are excluded). This false premise causes the plan to include removal of a non-existent mechanism, and acceptance criterion AC3 ('__init__.py is gate-visible (pinned row, not filename-exempt)') tests for a condition that already holds.
findings.location: Remediation section / AC3: '__init__.py is gate-visible (pinned row, not filename-exempt)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the Remediation bullet 'Also drop the __init__.py filename-pattern exemption (give it a pinned ceiling row instead) so it is gate-visible' with an accurate statement such as: '__init__.py files are already gate-visible (no filename exemption exists); if any are over 800 LOC, add a pinned ceiling row to the allowlist.' Revise AC3 to: '__init__.py files are covered by the gate; any over-cap instances have a pinned ceiling row in the allowlist.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and correct AC3: the current gate has NO `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption to remove — either drop AC3 or update it to describe what the intended gate-visibility change actually requires.
findings.finding: The plan's Remediation says 'drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption (give it a pinned ceiling row instead)' and AC3 says '`__init__.py` is gate-visible (pinned row, not filename-exempt)'. However, neither the test.yml gate, the gerrit-verify.yaml gate, nor `compute_over_cap_modules()` in the test file contains any `__init__.py` exclusion — the exemption the plan claims to remove does not exist in the codebase.
findings.location: Remediation section / AC3 / .github/workflows/test.yml line 183
findings.suggested_fix: Drop AC3 as written; if `__init__.py` files are currently under the 800-LOC cap they simply won't appear in any allowlist row — no explicit gate change is needed for them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether an `__init__.py` exemption actually exists in the current gate script (test.yml) before specifying its removal; if no exemption exists, drop or correct that remediation bullet so implementers aren't chasing a phantom.
findings.finding: The plan claims to 'drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption' as a remediation step, but no such exemption exists in the current gate script (test.yml lines 179-201 only filters `__pycache__`). This targets a non-existent mechanism, which will confuse the implementer and may cause a wasted search or a spurious no-op change.
findings.location: Remediation section: 'Also drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the sentence 'Also drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption (give it a pinned ceiling row instead) so it is gate-visible' and AC3 ('`__init__.py` is gate-visible (pinned row, not filename-exempt)'), or replace with a factual statement about what the current gate actually exempts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or correct the claim about a `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption: tool-verify confirms no such exemption exists in `test.yml` or `gerrit-verify.yaml`; the Remediation step should be dropped or replaced with the real work item it was meant to capture.
findings.finding: The plan's Remediation section references dropping the '`__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption' — implying a current gate rule that exempts `__init__.py` files — but tool inspection of both gate scripts (`test.yml` lines 179–201 and `gerrit-verify.yaml` lines 151–165) shows NO such exemption exists. The gate applies uniformly to all `*.py` files; there is no `__init__.py` carve-out to drop.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Also drop the `__init__.py` filename-pattern exemption (give it a pinned ceiling row instead) so it is gate-visible'

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `docs/architecture.md` as an edit target: update the 'source of truth' paragraph (lines 211-216) to reflect that the allowlist now carries per-file LOC ceilings and explain the ratchet semantics.
findings.finding: `docs/architecture.md` explicitly declares `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` as 'the source of truth for the over-cap set' and states 'exact line counts are deliberately NOT duplicated here — they drift every commit and a stale number is worse than none.' The plan's format change (adding LOC ceilings to the allowlist file) directly contradicts this documented design decision and the architectural narrative needs updating, but `docs/architecture.md` is not named as an edit target.
findings.location: Remediation section / docs/architecture.md lines 211-214

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies the 800-line ceiling is still enforced for new (non-allowlisted) files after the gate is rewritten.
findings.finding: The remediation body describes a deliverable — 'New non-allowlisted files keep the 800 rule' — that has no corresponding acceptance criterion. If this behavior regresses, no AC catches it.
findings.location: Remediation section: 'New non-allowlisted files keep the 800 rule'
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC5: 'A file not present in the allowlist that exceeds 800 lines causes CI to fail, unchanged from the current behavior.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the per-file ceiling is (a) the LOC at the time the file was ORIGINALLY grandfathered or (b) the file's LOC AT THE TIME THIS PLAN IS IMPLEMENTED, and state this explicitly in the plan.
findings.finding: The phrase 'ceiling = current LOC at grandfathering' is self-contradictory: 'current' implies today's LOC while 'at grandfathering' implies historical LOC. For attest.py the gap is 268 (original) vs 837 (today). AC4 requires files pass at their pinned ceilings, which is only true if ceiling = today's LOC — but then the ratchet provides no reduction pressure. An agent cannot determine which value to record without clarification.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'ceiling = current LOC at grandfathering'; AC4
findings.suggested_fix: State: 'ceiling = the file's current LOC as measured when this PR is merged (i.e., the LOC on the merge commit). Historical original LOC is not used.' This satisfies AC4 and sets a forward-only ratchet from today.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that only __init__.py files currently covered by the existing filename-pattern exemption (i.e., those that would otherwise exceed 800 lines) need a pinned ceiling row; all others fall under the standard 800-line rule by default.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify which __init__.py files should receive pinned ceiling rows. 'Give it a pinned ceiling row instead' is underspecified — a repo may have dozens of __init__.py files, most of which are trivially small and need no row; only those currently covered by the filename-pattern exemption need migration.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'drop the __init__.py filename-pattern exemption (give it a pinned ceiling row instead)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Remediation: 'Audit the current exemption scope: any __init__.py file whose LOC > 800 at merge time must receive a pinned ceiling row; all others are covered by the default 800-line gate and need no row.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the allowlist format migration (e.g. keep the old bare-filename gate path available until the new format is validated in CI, or document the exact revert commit procedure).
findings.finding: The plan changes the allowlist file format (bare filenames → `path <max-lines>` rows) and the test.yml gate logic simultaneously, but provides no rollback/back-out plan if the migration lands incorrectly (e.g. malformed rows, wrong ceiling values, or partial commit).
findings.location: Remediation section + Acceptance Criteria; .github/module-size-allowlist.txt format change

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief CLAUDE.md (or equivalent) entry documenting the allowlist row format (`path <max-lines>`), the ratchet policy (ceilings only move up by explicit reviewed commit), and how to grandfather a new file — so the contract survives beyond this ticket.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new CI governance pattern (per-file LOC ratchet with a structured allowlist format) but does not call for updating any persistent documentation (CLAUDE.md, ADR, or design doc) to capture the contract, format, or policy rationale for future maintainers.
findings.location: Remediation section / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to CLAUDE.md under a 'CI / Module Size Gate' heading: explain the two-tier rule (800-line default for new files; per-file ceiling for allowlisted files), document the row format, and state the policy that bumping a ceiling requires a deliberate reviewed commit referencing a justification.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the RETIRE behavior: specify whether the gate skips/warns on allowlist rows whose file no longer exists, and add a note that deleting a file requires removing its allowlist row.
findings.finding: The plan has no RETIRE path for allowlist rows whose corresponding source files are later deleted. Stale `path <max-lines>` rows will accumulate silently; if the gate errors on a missing file, CI breaks; if it ignores missing files, the allowlist becomes misleading dead config.
findings.location: Remediation — allowlist row format / test.yml gate logic
findings.suggested_fix: In test.yml gate logic: if an allowlisted file is absent from the working tree, emit a warning (or fail) and document in the plan that deleting a source file requires a corresponding allowlist-row deletion in the same PR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit migration step specifying the exact command/method used to measure current LOC for each allowlisted file and write the initial ceiling values into the allowlist.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify HOW the initial per-file ceilings are established for currently allowlisted files. AC4 requires them to 'pass at their pinned ceilings,' but no implementation step says who records the current LOC values or how (manual audit, script, CI-generated snapshot, etc.).
findings.location: AC4 — 'Current allowlisted files pass at their pinned ceilings' / Remediation paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add a remediation step such as: 'Run `wc -l <files>` (or the same counting logic used by the gate) on the current HEAD for each allowlisted file; record those values as the initial ceilings. Commit the updated allowlist as the first PR implementing this change.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the 'at grandfathering' LOC figures (attest.py=268, passes.py=589, det_floor.py=733, inbound_differ.py=754) against git history (`git log --follow -p -- <file>`) before using them as pinned ceilings; the plan currently cites these as fact but they are unverifiable from the current codebase.
findings.finding: The plan's Finding section asserts specific 'before allowlisting' LOC figures for the four named files: attest.py 268, passes.py 589, det_floor.py 733, inbound_differ.py 754. These historical figures cannot be verified from the current codebase (they require git history), making them unverifiable assertions under E4's fail-closed rule. If these figures are wrong, the growth percentages cited ('268->837 (+212%)') are wrong, which could undermine confidence in the remediation's urgency.
findings.location: Finding section: 'attest.py 268->837 (+212%), passes.py 589->839, det_floor.py 733->849, inbound_differ.py 754->802 all grew AFTER being allowlisted'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale for the `path <max-lines>` inline row format (e.g., no new tooling, extends the existing awk-based gate with minimal blast radius, keeps the allowlist self-documenting).
findings.finding: The plan states the chosen approach (inline `path <max-lines>` rows in the allowlist file) but provides no positive rationale for WHY this format was selected over structural alternatives — the absence of a stated rationale is a minor criterion gap per G6.
findings.location: Remediation section (approach description)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Remediation opening: 'The inline `path <max-lines>` format is chosen because it requires no new files or tooling — the existing `awk`/`comm` gate script needs only a minor extension, the allowlist remains human-readable and the single source of truth, and the blast radius is confined to one file and one shell step.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief justification (or a spike result) for why the bespoke `path <ceiling>` shell approach is preferred over existing per-file LOC enforcement tools (e.g., `wemake-python-styleguide`, `flake8-length`, or a Python helper reading a structured config), so reviewers can confirm the trade-off is intentional rather than overlooked prior art.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke `path <max-lines>` allowlist format parsed by a custom shell comparator, without considering whether existing lint/complexity tools (e.g., `wemake-python-styleguide`'s per-file size limits, `flake8-length`, or a structured Python config read by a small helper) already solve per-file LOC enforcement more maintainably. The novelty of a hand-rolled format + shell parser is not justified against these established alternatives.
findings.location: Remediation section — "Convert the allowlist from bare filenames to `path <max-lines>` rows"

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the gate's error behavior for a malformed allowlist row (missing ceiling field, non-integer ceiling) so the implementation is unambiguous — e.g., 'a row missing the ceiling field is a gate error that prints the offending line and exits 1 with an actionable message'.
findings.finding: The plan's Remediation specifies that 'The gate compares each allowlisted file against ITS OWN recorded ceiling; exceeding it fails CI' but provides no fallback/error behavior for a malformed allowlist row (e.g., missing ceiling field, non-integer ceiling, or extra whitespace). An incomplete row is a realistic failure mode during migration.
findings.location: Remediation section — gate behavior description; AC line 'test.yml fails when an allowlisted file grows past its ceiling'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
